Osteochondroma: The lesion arises from the metaphyseal region of the long bone, projecting away from the adjacent joint space.
A bony outgrowth is seen originating from the metaphysis, with its axis directed away from the growing end of the bone.
The exophytic lesion is centered in the metaphyseal area, maintaining continuity with the underlying medullary canal.
Radiographs show a pedunculated or sessile projection arising from the metaphysis of the affected bone.
The bony prominence is located in the metaphyseal region, typically adjacent to the growth plate in skeletally immature patients.
The lesion demonstrates sharply demarcated, sclerotic margins that blend smoothly with the adjacent normal cortex.

The margins of the bony projection are smooth and sclerotic, without evidence of ill-defined or aggressive permeation.
A thin, dense sclerotic rim outlines the base of the lesion where it merges with the underlying bone cortex.
The lesion exhibits well-circumscribed borders with no evidence of moth-eaten or permeative margin patterns.

Osteosarcoma: The osteosarcoma lesion is classically centered in the metaphysis of the involved long bone.
The tumor frequently originates near the growth plate with potential diaphyseal extension.
Isolated epiphyseal involvement is uncommon, helping distinguish it from epiphyseal neoplasms.
The lesion demonstrates a juxtaphyseal predilection that occasionally crosses the physis.

Multiple osteochondromas: Multiple osteochondromas typically arise from the metaphyseal regions of long bones, projecting away from the adjacent joint space.
The lesions are predominantly situated in the metaphysis of the femur, tibia, and humerus with characteristic orientation away from the growing end of the bone.
Exostotic projections in multiple osteochondromas are commonly observed in the metaphyseal areas of the pelvis and proximal limbs.
Radiographs reveal multiple bony outgrowths originating from the metaphyseal cortex of the upper and lower extremities.
The metaphyseal location of these sessile or pedunculated lesions is evident with their bases continuous with the underlying bone cortex.
Each osteochondroma demonstrates well-defined sclerotic margins with clear corticomedullary continuity with the parent bone.
The borders of the exostotic lesions are sharply demarcated and sclerotic, lacking any ill-defined or aggressive appearance.
Well-circumscribed margins with cortical and medullary continuity distinguish these benign outgrowths from more aggressive bone lesions.
The lesion margins appear smooth and sclerotic without evidence of permeative or moth-eaten border characteristics.

Dense ossification is visible within the matrix of the exostotic projections, demonstrating continuity with the underlying cortical bone.
Calcification within the cartilage cap may appear as irregular stippled densities overlying the bony stalk of the osteochondroma.
The lesion matrix shows mature trabecular bone pattern with ossification extending from the parent cortex into the exostosis.
Focal areas of increased density representing calcified cartilage are sometimes seen at the distal aspect of the osteochondromatous growths.
Homogeneous bony density throughout the lesion indicates complete ossification of the osteochondromatous outgrowths.
No true bone destruction is present; instead, the lesions demonstrate exostotic growth with preservation of underlying trabecular architecture.
Geographic bone remodeling may be seen adjacent to large osteochondromas due to pressure effects, but without permeative or moth-eaten patterns.
The underlying bone shows no evidence of aggressive destruction, maintaining a geographic pattern of remodeling where contact occurs.
Bone architecture beneath the exostosis remains intact with no moth-eaten or permeative destruction visible on radiographs.

The periosteum remains uninterrupted except for the normal cortical continuity at the base of each exostotic lesion.

Periosteal new bone formation is not visualized beyond the expected corticomedullary continuity of the osteochondromas.
The lack of elevated periosteal reaction or Codman triangle helps differentiate these lesions from malignant bone tumors.
No associated soft tissue mass is visible beyond the ossified exostotic projection on standard radiographic views.
The lesions remain confined to bony contours without evidence of extraosseous soft tissue extension.
Soft tissue planes around the osteochondromas are preserved without displacement or infiltration by a mass.
Extraosseous extension is not observed; the cartilage cap, if calcified, remains contiguous with the bony stalk.
Radiographs show no soft tissue component separate from the ossified exostotic lesions in uncomplicated cases.

Simple bone cyst: The simple bone cyst appears as a centrally located, radiolucent lesion within the metaphysis of a long bone.
A well-demarcated lytic area is situated in the proximal metaphysis immediately adjacent to the open physis.
The lesion occupies the metaphyseal region and may extend into the diaphysis in skeletally mature patients.
A solitary radiolucency is located in the metaphysis of the proximal humerus or proximal femur, typical sites for simple bone cysts.
The cystic lesion is positioned in the metaphyseal portion of the bone, characteristically sparing the epiphysis and articular surface.
The lesion demonstrates a narrow zone of transition with sharply defined, sclerotic margins.
A thin, continuous rim of reactive sclerosis outlines the perimeter of the radiolucent cyst cavity.
The borders of the lesion are smooth and well-circumscribed without irregular or infiltrative margins.

The cyst exhibits intact cortical margins with no evidence of cortical destruction or permeation.

The cyst causes gentle, symmetric expansion of the metaphyseal contour while maintaining cortical integrity.
There is symmetric widening of the bone diameter at the site of the radiolucent lesion without cortical disruption.
